summary:
Agent2 reasoned that among the provided choices, only potassium chloride lacks a recognized role in treating ectopic pregnancies due to lack of therapeutic indications related to uterine contractions or disruption of trophoblasts' function compared to others mentioned. Thus, \boxed{\textbf{potassium chloride}} was identified as non-applicable for managing an ectopic pregnancy medically. The focus remains strictly on identifying why one particular option stands out based solely upon current knowledge regarding these treatments’ efficacy against ectopic gestations without delving into broader discussions about their side effects or administration protocols. 

The summary maintains brevity while capturing essential details pertinent directly towards answering whether each substance serves clinically meaningful purposes within treatment regimens specifically designed around addressing complications associated with abnormal implantation sites outside normal intrauterine locations during early stages of human embryogenesis prior conception completion leading up until viability assessment post-fertilization event occurrence timeline considerations.
